author: @styleslider400
text: Hi Robert, have you done any videos on how to make a aluminum electrolytic capacitor? with some in depth info on the electrolytes that can be made for a electrolytic capacitor? such as does a certain type of electrolyte help improve dielectric constant of aluminum oxide or can a electrolyte be made to improve resistance of the oxide layer at high voltage. i would like to make a capacitor that can handle around the 300 - 500v mark or higher if possible while using a liquid electrolyte, and i am curious if there are any improvements that can be done with the electrolyte compared to what i am using (baking soda and De-ionized water "rimstarorg capacitor tutorial"), is pure 100% aluminum really needed? or can i get away with using a high grade aluminum like 6061 aluminum, as i want to make heavy duty anode and cathode plates and its a bit difficult for me to source 3mm thick pure 99% aluminum sheet. if you haven't done a video on how to make the basic electrolytic capacitor could you please make one. as you have so much experience in this area and it would be awesome just to hear what you have to say on this particular topic. i am currently having trouble with the test plates that i have in solution to form the oxide layer, i can create the oxide layer but its still leaking to much current 0.001 of an amp for a 25mm x 80mm plate, charged at 62v using a 62v dc power supply. the aluminum plates are made out of normal grade aluminum from the local metal shop so its not 99% pure. cheers

author: @technosaurus3805
text: Now try doing Styrofoam. Don't wash it in acetone first though... do it after for an ultralight copper sculpture.  It would also be an interesting way to get your current collector.  Just paint the copper again to get the internal cells of a stacked capacitor/battery.  For alkaline cells, just use nickel instead.

author: @William_Hada
text: Nice video! You can go one step further and plate over the copper with Nickel.  I have done this at home with great success. All you need is some pure Nickel metal and vinegar to make the nickel acetate electrolyte.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: awesome idea cheers

replies.author: @RWBHere
replies.text: After the Nickel, you could add a top layer of Chromium, but be careful; _Chromium salts are very toxic._  This is the correct procedure for adding a Chrome finish to any conductor, especially to Iron. Copper, then Nickel, and finally Chromium. If done properly each metal forms a good bond to the metal below it, and the Chrome finish will be the best-looking; a mirror finish which will last for many years.

author: @UpcycleElectronics
text:
I've been toying with the idea of trying through hole pcb plating. How does this product compare with the general DIY consensus of acrylic paint and graphite powder?

 You might benefit from doing a complete step by step tutorial specifically related to through hole plating, with a cutaway (or few) demonstrating the results under a microscope and the repeatability. Perhaps even creating a product kit with everything needed to try it. I imagine that would entail a very minor adjustment in your current lab supplies without making much investment in the endeavor. 
   Even with the current low cost Chinese PCB houses there is a lot of interest in DIY prototyping and processes right now, particularly in the Indian, Hispanic, and Slavic market. There are several new-ish YT channels that are doing really well by designing and building DIY etched prototypes and uploading them.
   Just something to think about ;)
Thanks for the upload. 
-Jake

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: awesome idea mate and i will try and do somethiing along the lines you suggest - though anything home done is never going to have the same results as commercial systems and repeatability is mor going to be an issue of how well a person does it as opposed to how well i do it - so i am thinking if it works at all it will be cool - as for repeating it to that level of success is going to be more down to an individuals skill as opposed to 'kit contents' - cheers

author: @19rocket64
text: brass is a bad idea....

replies.author: @amicklich6729
replies.text: Explain. Not being a jerk...just here looking for solid info.

replies.author: @19rocket64
replies.text: brass does conduct the electricity but is an alloy of zinc and copper.  Zinc has a much different electro negative #. This is why one metal is plated upon another in a specific order.  Just use the same metal, in this case copper wire to the object to be plated.

text: (polyethylene glycol), Antifreeze Isn't it ?  Only need 0.1% ? Not much at all then. Question: I use copper nitrate due to the fact its a byproduct of my silver refining and I have way lots of it. shouldn't it work the same in that as copper sulfate though ?

replies.author: @GreenCaulerpa
replies.text: actually PEG is not antifreeze, its corresponding monomer ethylene glycol can be found in antifreeze and breaking fluid

author: @crawfish069
text: Anode is the positive wire, right?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: the terms anode and cathode are confusing mate as they refer to the direction of current flow as opposed to the charge the electrode carries - 'An anode is an electrode through which the conventional current enters into a polarized electrical device. This contrasts with a cathode, an electrode through which conventional current leaves an electrical device. A common mnemonic is ACID for "anode current into device"' - Wikipaedia has a nice article on this - i took the quote from there. I find it best to think of what you want the ions to do - positive ions are attracted to the negative plate so you want what you want to plate to attract the positive metal ions and deposit there - therefore make your plating object negative and your donor metal plate positive

replies.author: @albertmueller6616
replies.text: Rob, Beautifully put. Yes it can be a bit confusing but it becomes much easier when you think of it that way. Trust me a dyslexic like me becomes quite confused with things like this and these little suggestions make it much easier to actually do the work. One more thing I would add , on a personal note: I find it works much better when you use as your Anode or positive electrode a metal of the same kind as you are trying to plate. For instance if you are copper plating in a copper+2 bath or solution use a copper Anode or positive electrode opposed to many time suggested titanium or stainless of what have you. I personally found I get better results with the finished product. Its just a guess but seems to me that over saturating the solution with copper ions before they all run to stick to the object being plated makes it work better somehow. One thing I can tell you for sure is that if your solution is something you want to & can store and use again that doing that will keep your solution from being used up so fast. In my case , the copper nitrate I use seems to last indefinitely so long as I am replacing the copper back to the solution I use for the plating. However you will find this eats the copper Anode away. These are just my findings and I have no scientific proof other than experience.

author: @diy-dash6103
text: Excellent work. Is the ink viscous enough to be used in an inkjet printer with a refillable cartridge? Imagine the possibilities being able to print and electroplate a flexible PCB.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it won't inkjet mate - the particle size is too big - the average nozzle is 45 micrometers across and the particle size is advised to be around 9 times smaller in order not to clog the nozzle so we need 5 micrometer particles - the problem there would be an increase in c plne trnsition points which would lower conductivity if the particles were that small.

replies.author: @sailingsolar
replies.text: Perhaps a specific larger sized orificed ink cartridges could be had that would allow an inkjet printer to be used to apply your ink when making cap's or batteries? It's a thought.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it is a thought and a good one - i am just not sure they do such things mate

author: @GreenCaulerpa
text:
I suggest using PEG [mw 10.000] (polyethylene glycol) and choline chloride as additive because it can prevent dendrite and hydrogen formation.

In the case of PEG a concentration of 0,1% is enough to completely prevent dendrites

replies.author: @GreenCaulerpa
replies.text: In case of PEG you can also find researches on this on google scholar

replies.author: @victorst5997
replies.text: Nice observation. Would you point out the paper, please? Thank you.

replies.author: @GreenCaulerpa
replies.text:
Victor ST 

https://ecs.confex.com/ecs/224/webprogram/Abstract/Paper24307/F4-2346.pdf

https://pubs.acs.org/doi/pdf/10.1021/acsami.6b16560

https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1002/celc.201800572

replies.author: @GreenCaulerpa
replies.text:
[Edit] I found a paper that mentions the hydrogen evolution suppression by choline chloride:

http://ma.ecsdl.org/content/MA2012-02/14/1790.full.pdf

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: there has been a lot of work on the use of deep eutectic solvents in electroplating - mostly by Lancaster university and a lot of additives will act to supress dendrites - really a study of bath formulation is needed if you are going to get serious about this - but for someone who just wants to have a go this bath works well enough at low current density

author: @What_I_Make
text: Very interesting, I personally invest in a current limiting power supply, loads out there and fairly standard on most cheaper power supplies now. Just set the current limit to 50mA or whatever and then crank the voltage to maximum. Copper electrodes for your batteries would be a bit complex, would be good to learn how to mask it so only a portion is plated.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: we did use a current limited supply on the video - the battery suggestion was for someone who didn't want to invest that much and just wanted to give it a go for fun - more serious platers really need to look at a good supply and bath formulation

replies.author: @albertmueller6616
replies.text: I have dabbled a bit with plating and found you will be surprised at the differences in results you can achieve just by changing a half volt or a minor adjustment in current to voltage. I find it just amazing what such a small change in either will cause to happen with the plating. I personally found my best results have been with voltage somewhere below 3v. DC and play with the current a bit starting low and work up slowly to get what I want.

replies.author: @What_I_Make
replies.text: Savvy, cheers :)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: awesome advice mate - thanks

replies.author: @RWBHere
replies.text:
We did our own Copper, Nickel and Gold plating at one of my places of employment. Even the plating of Gold was very dependent upon current density. 3V or less was usually used, with current limited by plating bath electrolyte concentrations and spacing between devices and the sacrificial electrode. Nothing much was written down in a public location, but the desired results were more down to experience of the operators than to textbook knowledge. Surprisingly consistent thicknesses and surface flatness could be achieved in that way. A long, slow plating time, coupled with scrupulously clean device surfaces always gave the best results. 

One cannot over-emphasise the importance of the best cleanliness (no debris, no grease, no handling of the initial surfaces after thorough cleaning, etc), and low plating current densities, in achieving good results.
